1: vayisu benei Yisra'el vayachanu be'arvot Mo'av: 'The Israelites traveled on and camped in the plains ('arvot) of Moab.' The 'arvot (pl. of 'aravah, steppe/plain) are the flat arid floodplains east of the Jordan opposite Jericho — a staging area for the Canaan entry narrative that will dominate the rest of the Torah. The phrase me'ever leYarden Yericho (across the Jordan from Jericho) is the geographical anchor of the final wilderness position; from here Israel will cross over. The entire Balaam section (Numbers 22-24) occurs at this liminal location — Israel on the threshold of the promised land, seen from outside by Balak and Balaam.
2: vayar Balaq ben Tzippor et kol asher asah Yisra'el la'Emori: Balak son of Zippor sees (vayar) what Israel did to the Amorites — the verb of perception opens the narrative. Balaq means 'destroyer/devastator'; Tzippor means 'bird' (the same word as tzipporah, Moses's wife's name). Balak is king of Moab (v4), though interestingly the narrative will name Midian's elders alongside Moab (v4,7) — a detail that will resurface in the Baal Peor incident (Numbers 25). His fear is based on intelligence about Israel's recent military victories over Sihon and Og (Numbers 21).
3: vayagor Mo'av mippenei ha'am me'od ki rav hu vayaqots Mo'av mippenei benei Yisra'el: Two verbs describe Moab's psychological state: yagor (was afraid/dreaded) and quts (was in dread/loathing). The root quts is strong — it can mean to be overcome with dread, even physically sickened. The phrase ki rav hu (because they are so numerous) echoes the Egyptian reaction in Exodus 1:9 (rav veatzum mimmenu, too many and powerful for us) — Moab's fear mirrors Pharaoh's, setting up a structural parallel between the Balaam narrative and the Exodus.
4: kha'asher yilchoch hashor et yerek hassadeh: The simile — 'the way an ox licks up the grass of the field' — is vivid and unusual: Israel is compared to an ox that devours everything it encounters. The Moabite leaders address the ziqnei Midyan (elders of Midian), suggesting a political alliance of Transjordanian peoples against Israel. Balak's request for Balaam's curse follows immediately from this fear — he cannot defeat Israel militarily and turns to supernatural means.
5: vayishlach mal'akhim el Bil'am ben Be'or Petorah asher al hanahar: Balaam son of Beor at Pethor 'which is near the River' (hanahar = the Euphrates, always the referent when unspecified in biblical Hebrew). Balaam's origin near the Euphrates places him in the region of Aram/northern Mesopotamia — the same region as Abraham's homeland (Haran), Laban, and Mesopotamian divination traditions. Bil'am ben Be'or: Beor is also the name of Bela son of Beor, king of Edom (Genesis 36:32; 1 Chronicles 1:43), raising the question of whether Balaam has Edomite connections, though the geographic description points to Mesopotamia. The phrase eretz benei ammo (land of his people) is ambiguous — 'his kinsmen' or possibly a place name Amaw (attested in Akkadian sources).
6: arah na li et ha'am hazeh ki atzum hu mimmeni: 'Curse (arah) for me this people.' Balak uses three curse terms across the narrative: arar (to curse, the strongest term, v6), qabav (to curse/injure, Numbers 22:11,17), and za'am (to denounce). The belief that professional cursing can determine military outcomes is well-attested in ancient Near Eastern sources (Egyptian execration texts, Mesopotamian cursing rituals). Balak's theology is that supernatural word-power can overcome superior military strength — a theology the narrative systematically dismantles. His final phrase, ki yadati et asher tevarekh mevorakh ve'asher ta'or yu'ar (because I know that those you bless are blessed and those you curse are cursed), grants Balaam enormous authority — which will be entirely redirected by YHWH.
7: vayelechu ziqnei Mo'av veziqnei Midyan uqesamim beyadam: 'The elders of Moab and Midian went, with divination fees (qesamim) in their hands.' The word qesamim is plural of qesem (divination, augury) — here it probably refers to the payment artifacts or tokens used in the divination trade rather than actual acts of divination. The term establishes Balaam's professional identity as a paid diviner, an occupation known throughout the ancient world. Alternatively, qesamim beyadam could mean they carried instruments of divination — physical tools of the trade as authentication of Balak's request.
8: vayomer alehem linu poh halayla vahashivoti etchem davar ka'asher yedabber YHWH elai: Balaam's response is remarkable: he says he will report ka'asher yedabber YHWH elai (what YHWH speaks to me). A Mesopotamian diviner who knows the divine name YHWH and receives communication from him — the text presents this without surprise, apparently accepting that YHWH communicates with non-Israelite prophets. This raises significant questions about the boundaries of YHWH's revelation. Balaam's willingness to wait for YHWH's word, rather than immediately accepting the payment, suggests a genuine prophetic constraint.
9: vayavo Elohim el Bil'am vayomer mi ha'anashim ha'eleh immakh: 'God came to Balaam.' The divine approach is initiated by God, not by Balaam's divination techniques — a detail that underscores YHWH's sovereign control of this episode. The divine question 'Who are these men with you?' is not a request for information but an invitation to narrative — the standard opener for divine interrogation (cf. Genesis 3:9 to Adam, 4:9 to Cain). Note the shift between Elohim (God) here and YHWH in v8 — both are used interchangeably in this narrative for the divine being who speaks to Balaam.
10: vayomer Bil'am el haElohim Balaq ben Tzippor melekh Mo'av shalach elai: Balaam reports Balak's message to God — a form of transparent prayer-report. The fact that Balaam addresses the Deity as haElohim (the God) — with the definite article — suggests either that he recognizes YHWH as the true God, or that this is simply the formulaic way a non-Israelite would refer to the deity speaking to him.
11: Balaam quotes Balak's message: 'A people has come out of Egypt and covers (yechasse, covers/conceals, from kasah) the face of the land.' The same metaphor as v4 but using a different verb. The phrase chakkah li oto (curse them for me) uses qabav (to curse, from an Aramaic root suggesting bore-through or wound); the same root appears in Job 3:8 and 5:3 in contexts of cursing and rooting out. Balak's revised request — 'perhaps I can fight them and drive them out' — shows military desperation as the underlying motive.
12: vayomer Elohim el Bil'am lo telekh immahem lo te'or et ha'am ki varukh hu: The direct divine prohibition: 'Do not go with them, and do not curse the people, because they are blessed (varukh hu).' The logic is decisive: YHWH's blessing on Israel makes it impossible for a curse to land. The benediction pronounced on Abraham (Genesis 12:2-3) — 'I will bless those who bless you and curse those who curse you' — is the theological foundation of YHWH's refusal. Balaam cannot curse what God has blessed — the operative principle is divine sovereignty over the power of speech-acts.
13: vayaqom Bil'am baboqer vayomer el sarei Valaq lechu el artzechem ki me'en YHWH letiti halekh immakem: Balaam complies with the prohibition and dismisses the first delegation. His report — 'YHWH has refused to let me go with you' — uses me'en (refuses, from ma'an, to refuse/decline), a strong term of willing refusal. The divine name YHWH appears explicitly in Balaam's speech — a foreign diviner who names YHWH as his divine constraint. This is a remarkable datum for understanding the text's view of divine revelation beyond Israel.
14: The Moabite princes return with a simple report: 'Balaam refused to come with us.' The verb is the same ma'an — but applied to Balaam rather than YHWH, as if the princes understood Balaam's refusal as his own choice rather than divine compulsion. Whether this reflects their interpretation or Balaam's phrasing to them (v13) is ambiguous.
15: vayoseph 'od Balaq shloch sarim rabbim venichbadim me'elleh: 'Balak sent again (vayoseph, and he added/repeated) more numerous and distinguished (nichbadim, heavy/weighty in honor) princes.' The escalation follows the standard diplomatic pattern of intensifying embassies — more representatives, more prominent, presumably with more silver. Nichbadim from kaved (heavy, honored) — the same root as kavod (glory/honor/weight); these are literally weighty men.
16: Balak's message through the second delegation escalates the request: 'Please do not let anything keep you from coming to me.' The imperative al na timana (please do not be withheld/prevented) uses mana' (to withhold/prevent), suggesting Balak is aware that Balaam claimed some external constraint. He is trying to remove that constraint by appealing more urgently.
17: kabbed akabedkha me'od: 'I will honor you very greatly.' The infinitive absolute construction kabbed akabedkha (honor-honoring I will honor you) is emphatic — the doubled kaved root intensifies the promise. Balak promises whatever Balaam asks. The offer of maximum payment frames what follows: Balaam's refusal to accept is not from lack of incentive but from genuine prophetic constraint (cf. v18).
18: lo ukhal la'avor et pi YHWH la'asot qetannah o gedolah: 'I cannot go beyond the mouth (pi) of YHWH to do anything small or great.' The phrase 'avar et pi (go beyond the mouth/command of) is an idiom for transgressing a divine command; pi YHWH (the mouth of YHWH) stands metonymically for the divine word. The formula 'small or great' (qetannah o gedolah) is a merism for anything whatsoever. This verse establishes Balaam's genuine prophetic constraint — he cannot manufacture a curse that YHWH has not sanctioned, regardless of the payment offered.
19: ve'attah shevu na vezeh gam attem halaylah ve'eda'ah mah yoseph YHWH dabber immi: Balaam asks the second delegation to stay overnight while he inquires further of YHWH — using yoseph (will add/say additionally). This verse is theologically complex: God already said no (v12). Balaam's reopening of the question has been read as (a) genuine prophetic uncertainty about whether the new situation changes anything, (b) moral ambiguity — he wants to go and is looking for permission, or (c) standard prophetic practice of re-inquiring. The narrative's irony is that YHWH will permit the trip (v20) but then be angry that Balaam went (v22).
20: vayavo Elohim el Bil'am laylah vayomer lo im liqro lecha ba'u ha'anashim qum lekh ittam ve'akh et hadavar asher adabber elekha oto ta'aseh: God's conditional permission — 'if these men have come to summon you, rise and go with them — but do only the word I speak to you.' The conditional im clause is significant: liqro lecha ba'u (if they came to summon you) may suggest that YHWH is taking Balaam at his word about the second embassy's purpose. The limitation — ve'akh et hadavar asher adabber elekha oto ta'aseh (only the word I speak to you, that you shall do) — is the same restriction as before, but now with permission to travel.
21: vayaqom Bil'am baboqer vayachavosh et atono vayeelekh im sarei Mo'av: 'Balaam got up in the morning, saddled his donkey, and went with the Moabite princes.' The donkey ('aton, a female donkey) is now introduced as a character in her own right. The saddling of one's own donkey (vayachavosh) is a mark of urgency or of personal action without servants — Abraham does the same before the Akedah (Genesis 22:3). The speed of Balaam's preparation and departure suggests eagerness, which may account for YHWH's anger in v22.
22: vayichar af Elohim ki holekh hu vayityatsev mal'akh YHWH baderekh leśatan lo: 'God's anger burned because he was going, and the angel of YHWH positioned himself in the road leśatan lo.' The phrase leśatan lo (as a satan/adversary to him) uses the root śatan (to oppose, to act as an adversary) — the same root as the noun śatan (adversary/accuser, used of the figure in Job 1-2 and Zechariah 3:1-2). Here it is a functional description: the angel acts as an opposing force in the road. The theological tension between v20 (permission to go) and v22 (anger that he went) has generated much discussion: either God is angry at Balaam's eagerness/motivation, or the narrative juxtaposes two divine responses that reflect the complexity of prophetic permission.
23: vatere ha'aton et mal'akh YHWH nitsav baderekh vecharbo shelufah beyado: The donkey sees the angel with a drawn sword — the same sword posture as the cherubim blocking Eden (Genesis 3:24 lahat hacherev hammithappekhet) and the commander of YHWH's army at Jericho (Joshua 5:13). The donkey's perception precedes Balaam's — a deliberate reversal: the animal sees what the prophet cannot. Balaam beats the donkey three times (Numbers 22:23,25,27) — the triple beating intensifies the irony of a famous seer being spiritually blinder than his pack animal.
24: The angel moves to 'a narrow path between the vineyards with a wall on each side' — the geography constrains the donkey's escape options and increases the pressure. The progressive narrowing of the path (field → walled path → no room to turn) mirrors Balaam's progressive entrapment by the divine will he is trying to circumvent.
25: vatilaches ha'aton el haqir vatilchotz et regel Bil'am el haqir vayoseph lehakotah: 'The donkey pressed (tilaches, to press/squeeze, from lachats) herself against the wall, crushing Balaam's foot against it.' Lachats is used in Exodus 3:9 and elsewhere for the oppression of Israel in Egypt — the same root. The donkey's foot-crushing is involuntary self-defense; Balaam's beating is the response of a man who cannot see what his mount is responding to.
26: The angel moves again to a place where 'there was no room to turn either right or left' — ein derekh limnot yamin ushemol. The progressively blocked path is a spatial enactment of the theological principle that YHWH's will has no available bypass: every route is closed, just as every route for cursing Israel will be closed.
27: vatere ha'aton et mal'akh YHWH vattirbats tachat Bil'am: The donkey lies down under Balaam — a final act of passive resistance. When beating no longer works, the animal simply stops. Tirbats (lay down) uses the ravats root, associated with an animal lying at rest (as in Genesis 49:9, Isaiah 11:6-7). Balaam's rage ('he struck her with his staff') is the rage of a man whose professional tools (words, spiritual perception) are entirely useless while his draft animal has superior discernment.
28: vayiftach YHWH et pi ha'aton vatomer leBil'am meh asiti lekha ki hikkitani zeh shalosh regalim: 'The LORD opened (vayiftach, from patach, to open) the mouth of the donkey.' The rare miracle of an animal speaking is paralleled in the narrative by the opening of Balaam's eyes (v31) — both openings are YHWH's act. The donkey's first words are a legal question: 'What have I done to you that you have beaten me three times?' The shalosh regalim (three times) will later echo in the donkey's argument (v30) that her past perfect service is proof of her current reliability. The same phrase shalosh regalim means 'three pilgrimage festivals' in Exodus 23:14 — an incidental pun of uncertain significance.
29: vayomer Bil'am la'aton ki hit'alalt bi lul yesh cherev beyadi ki atah haragtikh: Balaam's response — 'If only I had a sword in my hand, I would kill you now' — is dramatic irony: the angel has a drawn sword (v23), Balaam has none, and the donkey is the one who has saved his life (v33). Balaam's anger blinds him to the reversal: he threatens death to the creature that has been preserving him from death.
30: vatomer ha'aton el Bil'am halo anokhi atonkha asher rakhavta alai me'odekha hayom hazeh: The donkey's argument: 'Am I not your donkey (atonkha) that you have ridden (rakhavta) your whole life?' The phrase me'odekha (from your youth, or all along) is unusual — me'od normally means 'very/greatly' but here functions as 'for a long time.' The donkey appeals to their relationship history as evidence that her current behavior is anomalous and therefore meaningful. Balaam can only say 'No' — he concedes the donkey's prior reliability, which should raise a question he has not yet asked.
31: vayigal YHWH et einei Bil'am vayar et mal'akh YHWH nitsav baderekh: 'The LORD uncovered (vayigal, from galah, to uncover/reveal) Balaam's eyes.' The vocabulary of revelation: galah is the root of all the galut/exile language (uncovering = exposure/vulnerability) but also of divine disclosure (cf. the same root in galui einayim, eyes wide open, in Numbers 24:4). Balaam now sees what his donkey has seen all along and bows down — the seer is humbled by his blindness. The angel's drawn sword retroactively explains the donkey's behavior and vindicates her.
32: vayomer elav mal'akh YHWH al mah hikkita et atonkha zeh shalosh regalim hineh anokhi yatzati leśatan ki yarath haderekh lenaddi: The angel's rebuke uses the śatan root again — 'I came out as an adversary (leśatan) because your way is reckless before me.' The term yarath (headlong, precipitous) suggests recklessness or rushing — Balaam's eagerness to go was itself the problem, not just the going. The angel's statement reveals that Balaam's motivations, not just his actions, were under divine scrutiny.
33: vatire'ani ha'aton vatit negh lefanai zeh shalosh regalim ulai natah meacharai ki attah gam-otecha harasgti ve'othah hecheyeti: The angel's final word: 'If she had not turned away from me these three times, I would certainly have killed you, though I would have spared her.' The donkey's 'disobedience' to Balaam was obedience to the angel — it saved Balaam's life. The irony is complete: the beast Balaam wanted to kill was his unwitting protector. This functions as a narrative parable about Balaam's entire enterprise — what looks like resistance to his plans is actually divine preservation.
34: vayomer Bil'am el mal'akh YHWH chatati: 'Balaam said to the angel of YHWH: I have sinned.' The confession is immediate and appears genuine. The offer to return home is sincere — but the angel gives the same permission God gave in v20: go, but speak only my words. The entire episode seems to have functioned as a divine interrogation of Balaam's motivation, a stripping away of his prophetic pretensions before the oracles, ensuring that what follows is entirely YHWH's word, not Balaam's craft.
35: lekh im ha'anashim ve'efes et hadavar asher adabber elekha oto tedabber: The angel repeats the divine constraint: 'Go, but the word I speak to you, speak only that.' The word efes (but/nevertheless) introduces the limitation clause. This verse establishes the absolute prophetic constraint under which all four subsequent oracles will be delivered — Balaam speaks YHWH's word and nothing else, regardless of what Balak commands.
36: vayishma Balaq ki va Bil'am vayetse liqrattow el ir Mo'av: Balak goes out to meet Balaam at the city of Moab on the Arnon — the Arnon River was the northern boundary of Moab. The geography establishes that Balak meets Balaam at the outermost limit of his territory, the maximum advance to greet an honored guest.
37: vayomer Balaq el Bil'am halo shaloch shalacgti elekha liqro lakh lammah lo halachta elai: Balak's rebuke is tinged with both disappointment and political face-saving: 'Did I not urgently send for you? Why didn't you come immediately? Am I not able to honor you richly?' The infinite absolute shaloch shalachti (urgently/emphatically sent) mirrors Balaam's own emphatic speech. Balak assumes Balaam delayed because of insufficient honor offered — he still doesn't understand the divine constraint.
38: vayomer Bil'am el Balaq hineh bati elekha attah hayukhal adabber me'umah haddavar asher yasim Elohim befumi oto adabber: 'The word that God places (yasim, puts/sets) in my mouth — that I will speak.' The metaphor of God placing words in the prophet's mouth (yasim befumi) is the standard biblical idiom for prophetic inspiration: Exodus 4:15 (God to Moses about Aaron), Jeremiah 1:9 (YHWH touches Jeremiah's mouth), Ezekiel 3:3 (eating the scroll). Balaam presents himself as a passive receptor of divine speech — a claim the narrative endorses.
39: Balaam accompanies Balak to Kiriath-huzoth. The place name Qiryat Chutsot may mean 'City of Streets/Plazas' — a significant urban center in Moab. The journey to multiple vantage points (Bamoth-baal, Pisgah, Peor) across the oracles mirrors the visual-dominance strategy of Balak: show Balaam the people from the best angle to generate a curse. Each time YHWH redirects the visual encounter into a blessing.
40: vayizbach Balaq bakar vetson vayishallach leBil'am ulasarim asher itto: Balak sacrifices cattle and sheep — preparatory meals before seeking oracular speech. The sending of portions to the princes and to Balaam is standard Near Eastern banquet hospitality before a formal religious ceremony. The sacrifice is Moabite, not Levitical — the narrative does not comment on the appropriateness of the food but simply records the social ritual.
41: vayehi vaboqer vayiqach Balaq et Bil'am vaya'alehu bamot ba'al: 'The next morning Balak took Balaam up to Bamoth-baal.' Bamot (high places, cultic elevated platforms) were ubiquitous in Canaanite religion — bamot ba'al is specifically a Baal high place. From there Balaam 'could see a portion of the Israelites' (qetseh ha'am, the edge/extremity of the people). Balak's strategy of showing only a portion (rather than the full camp) will be varied at Pisgah (also partial, Numbers 23:13) and at Peor (overlooks the desert, not the camp). The visual frame matters for oracle purposes in ancient divination practice.
